---
title: "Cheatsheet · Estimands & validation"
subtitle: "Defining and checking causal quantities"
---

::: {.cheatsheet}

## Potential outcomes

For a binary treatment Z, each unit has $Y(0)$ and $Y(1)$; the individual effect
is $Y(1) - Y(0)$. We only ever observe one — the fundamental problem of causal
inference. Simulation solves it by *setting* both.

## The estimand zoo

| Estimand | Definition |
|---|---|
| **ATE** | $\mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0)]$ |
| **ATT** | $\mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0)\mid Z = 1]$ |
| **ATC** | $\mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0)\mid Z = 0]$ |
| **CATE(x)** | $\mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0)\mid X = x]$ |

Under effect heterogeneity, ATT ≠ ATC ≠ ATE. Define the estimand **before** the
model.

## The naive trap

The difference in means
$\hat\tau_{\text{naive}} = \bar Y_{Z=1} - \bar Y_{Z=0}$
equals the ATE only when treatment is unconfounded. Add a confounder and it
drifts — by a lot.

## Credence-style validation

[Realism first.]{.lead-in} Learn a covariate generator p(X | Z) from real data. &nbsp;
[Then inject truth.]{.lead-in} Set $Y(0) = h(X) + \sum_j \gamma_j U_j + \varepsilon$,
$Y(1) = Y(0) + \tau(X)$, with $\Pr(Z=1\mid U) = \text{logit}^{-1}(\kappa + \sum_j \rho_j U_j)$. &nbsp;
[Add bias last.]{.lead-in} An explicit g(X, Z) perturbs the *observed* outcome only.

::: {.pullquote}
Separate realism from truth: the covariate distribution comes from data, the
causal effects come from you. That is what makes a fair benchmark.
:::

## Layering several parameters

Credence is not limited to a single ATE — τ(x) and g(x, z) are already covariate
functions. Add more confounders, estimands, or a mediator and each is **one more
additive penalty**. This project layers them via a `SimulationSpec`.

:::

*Print for a handout.* Run scenarios live in the [app](../app.qmd).
